title: Cited 7 July 2026: ‘Impossible’ heat | Global ocean record | Climate change and the ozone hole - Carbon Brief
gist: Carbon Brief's Cited newsletter covers a US heat dome that killed at least 25 people and was ruled 'virtually impossible' without human warming, a record global ocean surface temperature, a WMO forecast of a strong El Niño, and a spotlight on how the Montreal Protocol's phase-out of CFCs and their replacements has substantially benefited the climate because those gases are themselves potent greenhouse gases.

insights.claim: At least 25 people died as a heat dome smothered the eastern half of the US, with more than 140 million people under heat alerts and over 20 states above 100F (38C).
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: Mass US heat mortality reinforces the physical-risk and adaptation-spending thesis for cooling, grid and health infrastructure.
insights.quote: At least 25 people died as a "heat dome" smothered the eastern half of the US

insights.claim: World Weather Attribution found the combined US heat and humidity would have been virtually impossible without human-caused warming.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: Formal attribution strengthens the legal and policy basis for climate liability and adaptation mandates.
insights.quote: the combined heat and humidity would have been "virtually impossible" without human-caused warming

insights.claim: Public Health France doubled its preliminary estimate of excess deaths from late-June extreme heat from 1,000 to more than 2,000, while Carbon Brief analysis put the figure at 2,700 heat-related deaths.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: Sharply rising European heat death tolls raise the political urgency of heat-adaptation legislation and spending.
insights.quote: doubled its preliminary estimate of the "excess deaths" from the extreme heat in late June, from 1,000 to more than 2,000

themes.theme: Ocean record and strong El Niño

insights.claim: Global average sea surface temperature reached a record 20.86C for the date on 21 June excluding polar regions, exceeding the 20.83C set on the same day in both 2023 and 2024, and has stayed at record levels every day since, per Copernicus.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: Record ocean heat is a leading indicator of hurricane intensity and marine ecosystem stress with direct insurance and commodity implications.
insights.quote: Daily SST for the global ocean, excluding polar regions, reached 20.86C on 21 June, exceeding the 20.83C reached on the same day in both 2023 and 2024

insights.claim: The WMO raised its forecast for the rapid emergence of a strong El Niño in coming months, with remarkable agreement between forecast models, warning it is likely to drive global temperatures higher.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: A strong El Niño skews the outlook toward record global temperatures and agricultural and energy-demand disruption.
insights.quote: there is a remarkable agreement between forecast models that ​this will be a strong El Niño

insights.claim: C3S director Carlo Buontempo said the record ocean conditions could indicate the beginning of a new phase, leading once more to uncharted territory.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: A regime-shift framing on ocean heat implies climate impacts may exceed historical models, raising tail-risk pricing.
insights.quote: could indicate the beginning of a new phase, leading, once more, to uncharted territory

insights.claim: The annual season when intense tropical cyclones occur has lengthened by 10-14 days per decade across the world since the 1980s.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: A lengthening intense-cyclone season extends the window of catastrophe exposure for insurers and coastal assets.
insights.quote: The annual season when "intense" tropical cyclones occur has lengthened by 10-14 days per decade across the world since the 1980s

insights.claim: The average lifespan of tropical cyclones in the north-east and north-west Pacific has shortened by 56 hours and 30 hours respectively over 1982-2024, compressing the time available for forecasting and disaster preparedness.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: Shorter cyclone lifespans reduce warning lead times, raising the value of faster forecasting and early-warning technology.
insights.quote: This shorter lifespan "compresses the time available for weather forecasting and disaster preparedness"

themes.theme: Ozone hole and climate co-benefit of Montreal Protocol

insights.claim: New research finds ozone depletion could have been detected as early as 1957 in the tropical upper stratosphere using modern satellite observations, before CFC use became widespread, when early depletion was caused by carbon tetrachloride.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: Illustrates how earlier detection technology could have accelerated regulatory action, a template for present-day monitoring investment.
insights.quote: We found that depletion could have been detected as early as 1957 in the tropical upper stratosphere, where natural variability is especially small
insights.locator: The ozone hole and climate change

insights.claim: Susan Solomon argues ozone depletion does not cause significant warming because the Earth is warmed much more by visible light than UV, correcting a common public conflation of the ozone hole with global warming.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: Debunks a persistent misconception that misdirects climate risk assessment away from actual warming drivers.
insights.quote: the Earth is warmed much more by visible light, UV doesn't really contribute, so ozone depletion doesn't cause significant warming
insights.locator: The ozone hole and climate change

insights.claim: The 1987 Montreal Protocol has substantially benefited the climate because CFCs and their HCFC replacements are strong greenhouse gases, so phasing out their production avoided both ozone depletion and significant warming.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: Frames refrigerant regulation as a high-leverage climate policy, relevant to the ongoing HFC phase-down market.
insights.quote: phasing out their production has not only avoided a lot of ozone depletion that would otherwise have occurred, it also had a big influence on global warming.

insights.claim: Hydrofluorocarbons, which are not ozone depleting but are potent greenhouse gases, are now being phased out under the 2016 Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: The Kigali HFC phase-down is a live regulatory driver for low-GWP refrigerant and cooling-equipment suppliers.
insights.quote: HFCs and similar replacements are now being phased out under the 2016 Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol.

insights.claim: From 2001 to 2015 northern Eurasia absorbed about 0.47bn tonnes of carbon each year, around one-third of the total global land carbon sink.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: Quantifying a single region's outsized carbon-sink role matters for carbon-budget accounting and offset validity.
insights.quote: northern Eurasia absorbed about 0.47bn tonnes of carbon each year – around one-third of the total global land carbon sink

insights.claim: A study argues the Paris Agreement's well-below 2C limit should be interpreted as a median peak warming of 1.6-1.8C rather than a 66-90% chance of staying below 2C to future-proof it.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: Reinterpreting the temperature goal tightens implied carbon budgets, raising the bar for corporate and national net-zero pathways.
insights.quote: it should be interpreted as a median "peak warming" of 1.6-1.8C, rather than a 66-90% chance of staying below 2C

insights.claim: A preprint warns that a strong weakening of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation would profoundly alter the climate-carbon cycle system, underscoring the need to account for AMOC risks in long-term climate assessments.
insights.speaker: Robert McSweeney
insights.so_what: AMOC tipping risk is a low-probability, high-impact tail that long-horizon climate and asset models increasingly need to price.
insights.quote: would "profoundly alter the climate-carbon cycle system", underscoring the "importance of explicitly accounting for AMOC risks in long-term climate assessments"
